lessons.title: 1.1 Why C++ in 2026? (The Invisible Giant)
lessons.content:
2026

The Foundation of Everything

Python and JavaScript run the web and AI apps. But what runs Python and JavaScript? C++. It is the language of high-performance infrastructure.

The Tech Stack Iceberg

Apps, Scripts, Websites (Python, JS)
AI Frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow)
Browsers (Chrome V8, WebKit)
Game Engines (Unreal 5, Unity)
OS Kernels & Drivers (Windows, Linux)

If you want to build the car, learn Python. If you want to build the engine, learn C++.

📈

HFT & Finance

High-Frequency Trading firms pay top dollar because C++ is the only language fast enough to beat the market by microseconds.

🏆

Competitive Programming

The massive STL library and raw speed make it the #1 choice for winning contests (IOI, ICPC, LeetCode).

🎮

Game Engines

Unreal Engine 5 uses C++ for gameplay logic. It gives you direct memory control for realistic physics and rendering.

lessons.title: 1.2 Setting Up Your Environment
lessons.content:
The Craftsman's Tools

You need three things: A Text Editor, a C++ Compiler, and a Build System.

GCC (g++)

GNU Compiler Collection

The standard on Linux. Reliable, ancient, powerful. The default for competitive programming.

Clang (clang++)

LLVM Project

Used by Apple and Google. Known for faster compile times and much better error messages.

MSVC

Microsoft Visual C++

The engine behind Visual Studio. Best if you are strictly on Windows developing desktop apps/games.

Recommended Setup: VS Code

Lightweight, fast, and industry standard.

C++
C/C++ Extension (Microsoft) Intellisense, Debugging, and Code Navigation.
Pr
Prettier / Clang-Format Auto-formats your code every time you save. Absolute must-have.

Build Systems: CMake

Don't memorize terminal commands. Use a recipe file.

CMakeLists.txt
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.10)
project(MyFirstApp)

set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20)

add_executable(app main.cpp)

This tells any OS: "I need C++20, and please turn 'main.cpp' into a program called 'app'."

lessons.title: 1.3 Your First Program: Anatomy & Execution
lessons.content:
The Anatomy of C++

Every C++ program, from a calculator to Unreal Engine 5, starts with these fundamental blocks. We are going to dissect a program line-by-line, digging into the Preprocessor, Namespaces, and the Main entry point.

main.cpp
#include <iostream>
The Preprocessor Directive:
Before compilation actually starts, the Preprocessor copies the entire content of the file iostream (thousands of lines of code) and pastes it right here. This gives you access to std::cout.
int main() { ... }
The Entry Point:
When you run your program, the Operating System calls this specific function. It must return an integer used as the status code.
std::cout << "Hello" << std::endl;
The Stream Operation:
std:: is a Namespace (like a surname). It prevents name collisions.
cout is the "Console Output".
<< is the insertion operator. It pushes data into the stream flowing to your screen.
; Semicolon ends the instruction. Forgetting this is the #1 error beginners make.
return 0;
Exit Status:
0 tells the OS "Success". Any non-zero value (e.g., 1 or -1) indicates an error happened.

Why std::?

Imagine you have a friend named "Cout". If you just yell "Cout!", it's confusing. std::cout specifies "The cout variable that belongs to the Standard family".

Avoid using namespace std;

While convenient, it pulls everything from the standard library into the global scope. If you name a variable count or data, it might collide with existing standard functions. Professional code uses explicit std:: or targeted using std::cout;.

The \n vs endl Debate

Both create a new line. But they are vastly different under the hood.

Feature\nstd::endl
TypeCharacterFunction (Manipulator)
ActionAdds newline charAdds newline + Flushes Buffer
PerformanceFastSlow (System Call)
Use CaseLoops / GeneralLogging / Crashes

What Happens When You Click Run?

It feels instant, but 3 distinct programs run in sequence.

1
Preprocessor Handles #include, removes comments.
2
Compiler Translates C++ to Machine Code (Object files).
3
Linker Combines Object files into one Executable (.exe).

lessons.title: 1.4 Variables, Types & Autos
lessons.content:
The Universe of Data Types

In Python or JavaScript, you just create variables. In C++, you are the architect of memory. You must decide exactly how much space every piece of data occupies. This control is what makes C++ the language of high-performance engines.

01 The Memory Grid (RAM)

Think of your RAM as a massive street with billions of mailboxes. Each mailbox holds 1 Byte (8 bits). When you declare a variable, you are reserving a block of these mailboxes.

Address 0x01
0
Address 0x04
Byte 1
Byte 2
Byte 3
Byte 4
← int (4 Bytes)
Address 0x08
'A'
← char (1 Byte)

Why size matters?

  • If you use a long long (8 bytes) for a small number like "5", you waste 4 bytes of RAM.
  • In a game with 1 million particles, wasting 4 bytes per particle = 4 MB of wasted RAM.
  • This is why C++ developers obsess over types.

1. The Integer Family

Whole numbers. No decimals. They come in different sizes based on how big of a number you need.

Type Size Range (Approx)
short2 B±32,000
int4 B±2 Billion (Standard)
long long8 B±9 Quintillion
The "Unsigned" Trick

By default, types are signed (can be negative). If you add unsigned prefix (e.g., unsigned int), you ban negative numbers but double the positive range (to 4 Billion).

⚠️ The Integer Overflow Trap

Imagine a car odometer at 999,999. If you drive one more mile, it wraps to 000,000. Integers do the same, but they wrap to their minimum negative value.

Code Memory Result
short x = 32767; 32767 (Max)
x = x + 1; -32768 (Min)

*This has caused rocket crashes and banking errors involved millions of dollars.

⚠️ The Precision Problem

Computers cannot represent decimal numbers perfectly (because they use base-2 binary).

double a = 0.1;
double b = 0.2;
// You expect 0.3, but...
cout << (a + b == 0.3); // Prints: False!
// Actual value is 0.30000000000000004
Rule: Never use == to compare floats. Check if the difference is very small instead.

2. Floating Point

Numbers with decimals.

Type Size Precision
float 4 B 7 digits (Low)
double 8 B 15 digits (Standard)

Always use double unless you are constrained by memory (like on a microcontroller).

3. Characters (char)

Computers only know numbers. A char is just a small integer (1 byte) mapped to a letter via the ASCII Table.

'A'
↓
65
'a'
↓
97
'0'
↓
48

Note: 'A' (Single Quotes) is a char. "A" (Double Quotes) is a String (an array of chars). They are NOT the same.

4. Booleans (bool)

The simplest type. Represents Truth.

  • true 1
  • false 0

Fun Fact: Any non-zero number is considered true in C++.
if (5) runs. if (-1) runs. Only if (0) fails.

Modern Initialization Rules (C++11+)

In the past, C++ let you do dangerous things like shoving a number with decimals into an integer variable (it would just silently delete the decimals). Modern C++ uses Curly Braces {} to prevent this.

The Old C-Style (Bad)
int width = 4.5;
// Compiles fine.
// width becomes 4.
// DATA LOSS!
The Modern Way (Good)
int width {4.5};
// COMPILER ERROR!
// "Narrowing conversion is not allowed"
// You are saved.
💡

Best Practice: Always use {} to initialize variables unless you have a specific reason not to. It is safer and consistent.

lessons.title: 1.5 Expressions, Operators & Precedence
lessons.content:
The Rules of Operations

Arithmetic

  • + Add
  • - Subtract
  • * Multiply
  • / Divide
  • % Modulus (Remainder)

Relational

  • == Equal To
  • != Not Equal
  • <, > Less/Greater
  • <=, >= L/G or Equal

Logical

  • && AND (Both true)
  • || OR (At least one)
  • ! NOT (Invert)

Common Traps & Rules

1
Integer Division truncates

5 / 2 is not 2.5. It is 2. The decimal part is discarded.

Fix: 5.0 / 2 (Force at least one side to be float)
2
Increment Post vs Pre

++i (Prefix) increments immediately.
i++ (Postfix) returns the OLD value, then increments.

int x = 5;
int y = x++; // y is 5, x becomes 6
3
Short Circuit Logic

C++ stops checking as soon as it knows the answer.

bool check = (false && crash_computer());
// crash_computer() never runs because False && Anything is False.

The Ternary Operator

A one-line if-else.

// condition ? true_val : false_val
int max = (a > b) ? a : b;

Compound Assignment

Syntactic Sugar.

x += 5;  // x = x + 5
x *= 2;  // x = x * 2
x %= 10; // x = x % 10

lessons.title: 1.6 Mini Lab: Solving Your First Problems
lessons.content:
Lab: Apply What You Know

Theory is useless without practice. Here are 3 challenges to test your skills with variables, I/O, and math.

Challenge 1: The Temperature Converter

Easy

Write a program that takes a temperature in Celsius and converts it to Fahrenheit.

Math Formula
F = (C * 9/5) + 32
Hint (The Integer Trap)

Be careful with 9/5. In C++, integer division 9/5 equals 1. You need to force floating point math. How?

Show Solution
#include <iostream>
int main() {
  double c;
  std::cout << "Enter Celsius: ";
  std::cin >> c;

  // Fix: Use 9.0 to force float division
  double f = (c * 9.0 / 5.0) + 32;

  std::cout << "Fahrenheit: " << f << std::endl;
  return 0;
}

Challenge 2: Odd or Even?

Medium

Ask the user for an integer. Tell them if it is Odd or Even. You haven't learned if statements yet? Use the Ternary Operator.

Logic Helper
number % 2

If remainder is 0, it is even.

Show Solution
// (condition) ? true_val : false_val
std::string result = (num % 2 == 0) ? "Even" : "Odd";

Challenge 3: Last Digit Trick

Hard

Write a program that prints ONLY the last digit of ANY integer the user enters. (e.g., Input: 12345 -> Output: 5)

Hint

The Modulus Operator (%) is your best friend here. What happens if you mod by 10?

lessons.title: 1.7 The Debugger: Your Superpower
lessons.content:
Stop Guessing. Start Seeing.

Beginners debug by adding cout << "here1" << endl;.
Pros use a Debugger. It lets you freeze time, inspect the brain of your program, and step through it line-by-line.

▶️
Watch Tutorial
1 int main() {
2 int a = 5;
3 int b = 10; ← PAUSED HERE
4 int sum = a + b;
5 return 0;
6 }
Variables
a 5
b random_junk_value
Why junk? We are paused on line 3. Line 3 hasn't executed yet. So b still holds whatever trash was in that memory address.

🔴 Breakpoint

Click to the left of a line number. The code will freeze exactly BEFORE running this line.

⤵ Step Over (F10)

Run the current line and move to the next one. This is what you use 90% of the time.

↓ Step Into (F11)

If the line is a function call, go INSIDE that function to debug it.

lessons.title: 1.8 Style & Best Practices
lessons.content:
Write Code for Humans

"Any fool can write code that a computer can understand. Good programmers write code that humans can understand." — Martin Fowler

Bad Code
int n = 86400;
int x = i * n;
// What is n? What is x?
Clean Code
const int SECONDS_IN_DAY = 86400;
int total_seconds = days * SECONDS_IN_DAY;
// Crystal clear.

1. Naming Conventions

Choose one style and stick to it.

  • Variables snake_case_variable
  • Functions CamelCaseFunction
  • Constants kConstantValue

2. Comments

Don't explain WHAT the code is doing (the code says that). Explain WHY it is doing it.

// Increment i by 1 (USELESS)
i++;
// Skip header row in CSV file (VALUABLE)
file.ignore(100, \n);

lessons.title: 2.1 Logic & Branching: The Art of Decision Making
lessons.content:
Controlled Chaos

A program without decisions is just a calculator. Control Flow allows your code to react to the world. We'll explore if, switch, and the hidden performance costs of bad decisions.

?

🌱 The Anatomy of Choice

Program Start
is_user_logged_in?
FALSE
TRUE
redirect_login() The "else" block
show_dashboard() The "if" block
Paths Merge
Program Continues

The if Statement

The bread and butter of logic. It evaluates a boolean expression. If true, it runs the block. 

if (health <= 0) {
    state = DEAD;
} else if (health < 20) {
    state = CRITICAL;
} else {
    state = ALIVE;
}
💡 Pitfall: The Assignment Trap
if (x = 5) is VALID code! It assigns 5 to x, returns 5 (true), and always runs. Always use if (x == 5).
Yoda Conditions (e.g., if (5 == x)) prevent this, as 5 = x is a compile error.

The switch Statement

Optimized for optimizing a single variable against constants. Faster than if-else chains for many cases (jump tables). 

switch (input) {
    case 'w': move_forward(); break;
    case 'a': turn_left(); break;
    case 's': move_back(); break;
    case 'd': turn_right(); break;
    default:  beep();
}
💡 Feature: Fallthrough
Without break, execution flows to the next case.
case 'a': case 'A': executes for both.

🔧 Under the Hood: Branch Prediction

Performance Critical

Modern CPUs are pipelines. They fetch instructions miles ahead of execution. When they see an if, they guess the outcome to keep the pipeline full.

Scenario A: Sorted Data

Values: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6...
if (val > 50) ...

The pattern is typically TTTTTFFFFFF. The CPU learns this instantly.
Result: Zero Stalls. Maximum Speed.

Scenario B: Random Data

Values: 82, 4, 19, 99, 1...
if (val > 50) ...

The pattern TFFTFTT is unpredictable. The CPU guesses wrong ~50% of the time.
Result: Pipeline Flush. 6x Slower.

lessons.title: 2.2 Loops & Iteration: The Engines of Code
lessons.content:
The Infinite Cycle

Computers excel at repetition. Loops allow us to run the same block of code millions of times per second. We will master the for, while, and modern range-based for.

⚙️

The 'For' Loop

Best when you know the exact number of iterations.

for (int i=0; i<10; i++) {
  run();
}
1. Init
2. Condition
3. Body
4. Increment

The 'While' Loop

Best when waiting for a state change.

while (player.isAlive()) {
  gameLoop();
}
Checks condition before the body runs. Might run 0 times.

The 'Do-While' Loop

Guaranteed to run at least once.

do {
  input = read();
} while (input != -1);
Checks condition after the body runs. Good for inputs.
Modern C++ (C++11)

Range-Based For Loops

Stop worrying about iterators and bounds checks. Read this as "For every item in collection".

The Old Way (Error Prone) 
std::vector<int> nums = {10, 20, 30};
for (int i = 0; i < nums.size(); ++i) {
    std::cout << nums[i] << " ";
}
The Modern Way (Clean) 
std::vector<int> nums = {10, 20, 30};
for (int num : nums) {
    std::cout << num << " ";
}
💡
Performance Tip: Use const auto& to avoid copying complex objects.
for (const auto& player : players) = "Look at every player, don't copy them, don't modify them."

Breaking the Cycle

break

Immediately exits the loop. Useful for "Search and Stop".

continue

Skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next one. Useful for filtering.

goto

Jumps to a specific label. Avoid at all costs. Creates spaghetti code.

lessons.title: 2.3 Functions & The Stack: Memory Deep Dive
lessons.content:
The Stack Frame

Functions are not just code jumps. They are memory operations. Every time you call a function, a new "world" is created on the RAM. This is The Stack.

Code View

void B(int x) {
    int y = x + 1;
    // We are here
}

void A() {
    int val = 10;
    B(val);
}

int main() {
    A();
    return 0;
}

RAM View (Stack)

main()
A() Frame val = 10 Return Addr
B() Frame x = 10 y = 11
Stack Grows ↓ (Lower Addresses)

The Cost of Recursion

Recursion is when a function calls itself. Each call creates a NEW Stack Frame.

void forever() {
  forever();
}
→

Stack Overflow

You incorrectly fill up the entire reserved stack memory (usually ~1MB). The OS kills your program instantly.
Solution: Always ensure a "Base Case" to stop the recursion.

Function Terminology

Declaration (Signature) int add(int a, int b);

"Hey Compiler, this function exists somewhere. Trust me." (Usually in headers)

Definition (Body) int add(int a, int b) { return a+b; }

"Here is the actual code." (Usually in .cpp files)

Parameters vs Arguments

Params: Variables in func definition (a, b).
Args: Values passed when calling (10, 20).

lessons.title: 2.4 References vs Pointers: Data Transport
lessons.content:
Moving Heavy Objects

If you want to show your friend a house, you don't pick up the house and move it to them. You give them the address. C++ gives you two ways to do this: Pointers and References.

Mechanic
Real World Metaphor
C++ Concept
Value
Xeroxing a document and giving the copy. If they draw on it, your original is safe.
void f(int x)
Reference
Sharing a Google Doc link. If they edit it, you see the changes.
void f(int &x)
Pointer
Writing the GPS coordinates on a napkin. You can lose the napkin (null) or write garbage coordinates (crash).
void f(int *x)

The Golden Rule: const ref

Copying large objects (like a 1MB Image or a long String) is slow. Passing by reference is fast, but dangerous (function might change it).

Solution: const Type&

"Here is a link to the Google Doc (Reference), but in 'View Only' mode (const)."

// BAD (Slow copy)
void printImage(Image img);

// GOOD (Fast & Safe)
void printImage(const Image& img);

Why use Pointers at all?

References are safer and easier. But Pointers have two superpowers:

1. Nullability

A reference MUST refer to something. A pointer can be nullptr (pointing to nothing). Useful for "optional" data.

2. Reassignability

A reference is bound for life. A pointer can allow you to point to Object A, then later point to Object B.

lessons.title: 2.5 Scope, Lifetime & Storage Classes
lessons.content:
Where do variables live?

Understanding variable lifetime is key to avoiding bugs.

1

Automatic (Stack)

Default for local variables.

{
  int x = 10; // Born here
} // Dies here (Popped from Stack)
2

Static (Global Data)

Lives for the ENTIRE program duration.

void count() {
  static int total = 0; // Init once
  total++; // Remembers value between calls!
}

⚠️ Variable Shadowing

When an inner scope variable has the same name as an outer scope one. The inner one "shadows" (hides) the outer one. 

int x = 10;
if (true) {
    int x = 5; // Shadowing!
    cout << x; // Prints 5
}
cout << x; // Prints 10
Avoid this. It creates confusing bugs. Enable compiler warnings (-Wshadow).

lessons.title: 2.6 Headers & Compilation: The Build Pipeline
lessons.content:
From Text to Matrix

How does your English-like C++ code turn into binary 1s and 0s? It's a 3-step assembly line.

Preprocessor
Handles #include, #define. Copy-pastes text.
→
Compiler
Checks syntax. Converts .cpp to .o (Object files).
→
Linker
Stitches .o files together into .exe. Resolves symbols.

The Linker Puzzle

C++ files (.cpp) are compiled separately. They don't know about each other.
Header files (.h) act as a "Promise". They tell file A that a function exists in file B (later).

  • .h (Header): Declarations (What it is). Shared.
  • .cpp (Source): Definitions (How it works). Private.
// math.h
int add(int a, int b);
// main.cpp
#include "math.h"
add(1, 2); // Compiler: "I know 'add' exists!"
// math.cpp
int add(int a, int b) { return a+b; }

One Definition Rule (ODR)

If you accidentally include the same header twice (e.g., A includes B, B includes C, A includes C), the compiler sees duplicate definitions and crashes.

#pragma once

Put this at the top of every .h file. Solved.

🛡️

lessons.title: 2.7 Namespaces and Scope Resolution
lessons.content:
📦

Namespaces

As your project grows, names collide. Two libraries might both have a Physics class. Namespaces act like folders for your code names.

Defining

namespace engine {
    void init() { ... }
}

namespace game {
    void init() { ... }
}

Using

int main() {
    engine::init();
    game::init();
}

The :: Operator

This is the "Scope Resolution Operator". It tells the compiler exactly where to look.

  • std::cout -> Look in 'std' namespace for 'cout'.
  • ::x -> Look in the Global Scope for 'x' (ignoring locals).

⛔ Polluting the Namespace

using namespace std;

Never do this in header files! It forces the entire C++ standard library (thousands of names) into the global scope for ANY file that includes your header. It leads to mysterious compilation errors.

lessons.title: 2.8 Mini Project: Math Utils
lessons.content:
Mission Brief

Build your first reusable library.

Objective

You will implement a multi-file project. You'll create a flymath namespace, split declarations and definitions, and compile multiple files together.

math_utils.h
#pragma once

namespace flymath {
  long long factorial(int n);
  bool isPrime(int n);
}
math_utils.cpp
#include "math_utils.h"

namespace flymath {
  long long factorial(int n) { ... }
  bool isPrime(int n) { ... }
}
main.cpp
#include <iostream>
#include "math_utils.h"

int main() {
  std::cout << flymath::factorial(5);
}

Implementation Details

  • Factorial: Handle 0! = 1. Use long long to prevent overflow up to 20!.
  • IsPrime: Loop from 2 to sqrt(n). Return false if divisible.
  • Compile: You must tell GCC to stitch them together:
    g++ main.cpp math_utils.cpp -o app

lessons.title: 3.1 Stack vs Heap: Theoretical Foundations
lessons.content:
Memory Models & Layouts

The Process Address Space: When a program runs, the OS allocates a virtual address space. This is divided into segments:

  • Text Segment: Read-only executable code instructions.
  • Data/BSS: Global and static variables.
  • Stack: Local variables, function return addresses (Grows Downwards).
  • Heap: Dynamic memory, managed by allocators (Grows Upwards).

The Stack (LIFO)

Mechanism: Managed by the CPU's Stack Pointer (SP) register. Allocation is a simple subtraction (SP -= size). Deallocation is addition (SP += size).

0x7FFF0008: Return Addr
0x7FFF0004: int x = 10
0x7FFF0000: int y = 20
⬆ Stack Pointer (SP) ⬇
  • Cache Locality: Excellent (Contiguous).
  • Thread Safety: Implicit (Thread-Local).
  • Limit: ~8MB (Stack Overflow if exceeded).
S

The Heap (Free Store)

Mechanism: Managed by the Runtime (malloc/free). Requires navigating a Linked List of free blocks (Free List) to find space.

0x1000
Obj
0x1008
Obj
Obj
Obj
  • fragmentation: Holes appear over time.
  • Cost: Expensive (Syscalls like brk or mmap).
  • Thread Safety: Requires Locks (Mutex).
H

lessons.title: 3.2 Review: Pointers & References
lessons.content:
Indirection Essentials

Formal definitions and memory mechanics.

Property Pointer (T*) Reference (T&)
Definition A variable holding a memory address. An alias for an existing object.
Nullability Can be nullptr. Must be valid upon initialization.
Arithmetic Supported (ptr + 1 moves sizeof(T)). Not Supported.
Reassignment Can point to new targets. Cannot be rebound.

Deep Dive: Pointer Arithmetic

If int* p = 0x1000 and sizeof(int) == 4:

  • p + 1 is NOT 0x1001.
  • p + 1 IS 0x1004 (Base + 1 * sizeof(int)).
0x1000
0x1004
0x1008

lessons.title: 3.3 C-Style Arrays & The Danger Zone
lessons.content:
The Buffer Overflow

C-style arrays decay into raw pointers. They do not know their own size. This is the #1 cause of security vulnerabilities in history.

int arr[3] = {1, 2, 3};
arr[100] = 999; // CRIMINALLY DANGEROUS

The compiler allows this! You are overwriting other secrets in RAM.

Decay to Pointer

When passed to a function, an array loses its safety.

void func(int arr[]) {
    // sizeof(arr) is NOT array size!
    // It is sizeof(int*) = 8 bytes.
}

Modern Fix: std::array

Zero-overhead wrapper that knows its size.

#include <array>
void func(std::array<int, 3>& arr) {
    // Safe. Size is baked into type.
}

lessons.title: 3.4 Const Correctness: The Engineer's Contract
lessons.content:
Read-Only Safety

const is not just for variables. It is for API Design.

const Data

const int x = 5;

I promise not to change x.

x = 6; // Error

const Pointer

const int* p;

I promise not to change what I point to.

*p = 6; // Error

const Method

int get() const;

I promise this function won't change the object.

Pro Tip: Read declarations from Right-to-Left.
int const * p -> "p is a pointer to an integer that is constant."

lessons.title: 3.5 Dynamic Memory (new/delete) vs RAII
lessons.content:
The Old World vs The New World

Manual Management (C++98)

void oldWay() {
    int* p = new int(5);
    
    if (error) return; // MEMORY LEAK!
    
    delete p;
}

You must remember to delete on EVERY exit path. This is impossible in large systems.

RAII (Modern C++)

void newWay() {
    std::unique_ptr<int> p = 
         std::make_unique<int>(5);
    
    if (error) return; // SAFE.
    
    // Destructor called auto-magically.
}

The resource is tied to the scope. When the scope ends (for any reason), cleanup happens.

lessons.title: 3.6 Unique Ownership: std::unique_ptr
lessons.content:
std::unique_ptr<T>

Scope A
Ptr
0xHeapData
➝
Scope B
Ptr (Moved)
0xHeapData
Copy Semantics Deleted:
unique_ptr(const unique_ptr&) = delete;

lessons.title: 3.7 Shared Ownership: std::shared_ptr
lessons.content:
Reference Counting Architecture

Understanding the Control Block.

Control Block (Heap)

strong_ref
3
weak_ref
0
deleter
default_delete
P1
P2
P3
All 3 pointers simulate ownership of the same resource.
Critical Performance Note:
Incrementing the reference count is Atomic (Thread-Safe). This introduces a small overhead compared to raw pointers or unique_ptr.

lessons.title: 3.8 Lab: Build 'SmartPtr' (Academic)
lessons.content:
Lab: Implement SmartPtr

Master the Rule of Five and Move Semantics.

Project Specification

You will implement a simplified std::unique_ptr. This requires understanding how to manage ownership transfer and prevent resource leaks.

Required Features

  • 1. Template T Class must accept any type: SmartPtr<int>, SmartPtr<Player>.
  • 2. Copy Construction (Delete!) SmartPtr(const SmartPtr&) = delete;.
  • 3. Move Semantics Implement Move Constructor and Move Assignment Operator.

Starter Code

template <typename T>
class UniquePtr {
private:
    T* ptr;
public:
    explicit UniquePtr(T* p = nullptr) : ptr(p) {}
    
    // Destructor
    ~UniquePtr() { delete ptr; }

    // 1. DISABLE COPY
    UniquePtr(const UniquePtr&) = delete;
    UniquePtr& operator=(const UniquePtr&) = delete;

    // 2. ENABLE MOVE (Transfer Ownership)
    UniquePtr(UniquePtr&& other) noexcept : ptr(other.ptr) {
        other.ptr = nullptr;
    }
};

lessons.title: 4.1 Vector: The Contiguous Array
lessons.content:
Definition & Theory

Definition: std::vector is a sequence container that encapsulates dynamic size arrays. It manages its storage automatically, expanding as needed.

  • Contiguous Memory: Elements are stored adjacent to each other in memory.
  • Random Access: Support for O(1) access via index [].
  • Dynamic Growth: When full, it allocates a larger block (usually 2x), copies elements, and deletes the old block.

Visualizing Reallocation

Pushing 1 element when full triggers a massive copy operation. This is why reserve() is critical.

Capacity: 4 (FULL)
A
B
C
D
push_back('E') -> NEW ALLOC (Cap: 8)
DELETED
A
B
C
D
A
B
C
D
E

lessons.title: 4.2 String Theory: More than char arrays
lessons.content:
std::string Basics

Safely manipulating text without buffer overflows.

Short String Optimization (SSO)

If a string is short (usually < 16 or 24 chars), it is stored directly inside the object on the stack to avoid heap allocation.

Operations

  • s + " World" // Concatenation
  • s.substr(0, 5) // Substring
  • s.find("el") // Search (returns npos if fail)

std::string_view (C++17)

A read-only window into a string. Zero copies. High performance. 

void print(std::string_view sv) {
    // No allocation. Just a pointer + size.
    cout << sv;
}

lessons.title: 4.3 std::array & std::deque
lessons.content:
std::array

Stack

A fixed-size array that doesn't decay to a pointer. It lives on the stack (usually). 

std::array<int, 5> arr = {1, 2, 3};
// Size must be known at compile time!

std::deque

Hybrid

"Double Ended Queue". Fast insertion at BOTH front and back.

It is NOT contiguous. It is a collection of fixed-size chunks.

lessons.title: 4.4 Tuple, Pair & Structured Bindings
lessons.content:
Returning Multiple Values

Structured Bindings (C++17)

Unpack tuples, pairs, and structs directly into variables.

The Old Way (Gross)
std::pair<int, int> p = {10, 20};
int x = p.first;
int y = p.second;
The Modern Way (Clean)
std::pair<int, int> p = {10, 20};
auto [x, y] = p; // Magic!

lessons.title: 4.5 Iterators: The Bridge
lessons.content:
The Universal Interface

Iterators abstract away the container. An algorithm like sort doesn't know it's sorting a vector. It acts on iterators.

Input Iterator
Read Once (Stream)
Forward Iterator
Read/Write logic
Random Access
Jump +N (Vector/Deque)

lessons.title: 4.6 Invalidation: When Pointers Lie
lessons.content:
Iterator Invalidation

If a vector reallocates (grows), all pointers and iterators to its elements become Dangling Pointers. Using them crashes the app.

auto it = vec.begin();
vec.push_back(10); // ALLOCATES NEW BLOCK
cout << *it; // CRASH! 'it' points to old freed memory.

lessons.title: 4.7 Algorithms: Sort, Find, Binary Search
lessons.content:
std::sort

Introsort (Mix of QuickSort, HeapSort, InsertionSort). O(N log N).

std::find

Linear Scan. O(N).

std::binary_search

Requires sorted range. O(log N).

The "Algorithm" Header

Never write your own sort or search loops unless it's homework. The STL versions are optimized for cache locality and edge cases.

lessons.title: 4.8 Lab: Building a CP Toolkit
lessons.content:
Lab: The Competitive Template

Implement a macro-free, modern C++ template for speed.

// 1. Fast I/O
std::ios::sync_with_stdio(0); std::cin.tie(0);
// 2. Type Aliases
using ll = long long;
using vi = std::vector<int>;
// 3. Universal Printer
Use templates to print any container.

lessons.title: 5.1 Associative Containers: Maps & Sets
lessons.content:
Ordered Trees

std::set and std::map are typically implemented as Red-Black Trees.

  • Ordered: Iterating goes from Smallest to Largest.
  • Complexity: O(log N) for Insert, Delete, Search.
  • Unique Keys: std::set and std::map do not allow duplicates. Use multi variants if needed.
50
20
70

lessons.title: 5.2 Unordered Containers: Hash Maps
lessons.content:
The Hash Table

std::unordered_map aims for O(1) access. It uses a Hash Function to map a Key to a Bucket Index.

Warning: In competitive programming, standard hash functions can be hacked/collided. Use a custom hash.
hash("Apple") % 4 == 1
hash("Banana") % 4 == 3
0
1
"Apple"
2
3
"Banana"

lessons.title: 5.3 Adaptors: Stack, Queue, Priority Queue
lessons.content:
Container Adaptors

These are not new containers. They are wrappers around existing ones (like Deque or Vector) that restrict the interface.

Stack (LIFO)

Underlying: Deque/Vector
push()
pop()
top()

Queue (FIFO)

Underlying: Deque/List
push()
pop()
front()

Priority Queue

Underlying: Vector (Heap)
push()
pop() // Removes Max
top() // Gets Max

lessons.title: 5.4 Small String Optimization (SSO)
lessons.content:
The Union Optimization

How std::string avoids heap allocation for short strings.

Internal Memory Layout (64-bit)

Scenario A: "Hello" (5 chars) SSO Active
'H'
'e'
'l'
'l'
'o'
\0
Unused Buffer
Data stored directly on the Stack inside the object. Zero Heap Allocations.
Scenario B: "A very long string..." (> 23 chars) Heap Fallback
Ptr (0x5000)
→
Heap Buffer: "A very long string..."
The internal buffer is reinterpreted as a {pointer, size, capacity} struct.
Key Insight: sizeof(std::string) is constant (usually 32 bytes). It uses a union to switch between the Local Buffer (SSO) and the Remote Pointer (Heap) based on string length.

lessons.title: 5.5 Allocators & PMR
lessons.content:
The Allocator Protocol

Definition: An allocator is an object responsible for encapsulating memory management strategies. It decouples allocation (getting memory) from construction (creating objects).

Standard Requirements (C++17)

  • T* allocate(size_t n) Allocates storage for n objects of type T. Does NOT construct.
  • void deallocate(T* p, size_t n) Deallocates storage. Does NOT destruct.

lessons.title: 5.6 Performance Analysis & Tradeoffs
lessons.content:
Choosing the Right Tool

Container Access Insert (End) Insert (Mid) Cache Friendliness
Vector O(1) O(1) Amortized O(N) A+ (Best)
Deque O(1) O(1) O(N) B (Chunked)
List O(N) O(1) O(1) F (Pointer Chasing)
Unordered Map O(1) Avg O(1) Avg O(1) Avg F (Node based)

lessons.title: 5.7 Lab: HFT Arena Allocator
lessons.content:
Lab: Linear Arena Allocator

Implementing O(1) memory allocation for High-Frequency Trading.

Project Specification

You will implement a "Linear Allocator" (also known as a Monotonic Buffer or Arena). It pre-allocates a large block and hands out chunks by simply incrementing a pointer.

Core Requirements

  • 1. Pre-Allocation Constructor allocates a large std::vector<char> or new char[] as the arena.
  • 2. Pointer Bump allocate(n) returns the current offset and increments it. O(1).

lessons.title: 5.8 Lab: In-Memory Game Leaderboard
lessons.content:
Lab: Real-Time Leaderboard

Maintain top players using Sets and Maps.

Design a system that tracks millions of player scores and can return the "Top 10" in real-time.

Challenge

Using a vector and sorting it every time is O(N log N). Too slow.
Use a std::set (Balanced Tree) to keep it sorted automatically in O(log N).

lessons.title: 6.1 Time & Space Complexity
lessons.content:
Big O Notation

How does your code scale? In CP, you have 1 second (approx 10^8 operations).

O(1) - Constant
Array access, Stack push/pop.
O(log N) - Logarithmic
Binary Search, Set/Map operations.
O(N) - Linear
Looping through an array.
O(N^2) - Quadratic
Nested loops. (Limit: N <= 5000).

lessons.title: 6.2 Two Pointers Technique
lessons.content:
Squeezing the Array

Solve problems like "Find pair with sum X" in O(N) instead of O(N^2) by moving pointers from both ends.

L →
1
3
5
8
← R
while (L < R) {
    int sum = arr[L] + arr[R];
    if (sum == target) return true;
    if (sum < target) L++;
    else R--;
}

lessons.title: 6.3 Sliding Window
lessons.content:
The Worm Technique

Find the maximum sum subarray of size K. Don't recompute the sum from scratch! Just Add New, Remove Old.

2
5
1
9
2
5
1
Sum = 8

lessons.title: 6.4 Prefix Sums
lessons.content:
Range Queries in O(1)

Calculating sum(L, R) naively is O(N). With Prefix Sums, it's instant.

// Precomputation O(N)
pref[i] = pref[i-1] + arr[i];
// Query O(1)
sum(L, R) = pref[R] - pref[L-1];

lessons.title: 6.5 Sorting Tricks & Custom Comparators
lessons.content:
Mastering std::sort

Sort Descending: sort(v.rbegin(), v.rend());
Custom Structs: sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](const Item& a, const Item& b) {
  return a.price < b.price; // Cheapest first
});

lessons.title: 6.6 Binary Search on Answer
lessons.content:
Monotonic Functions

Binary Search isn't just for finding numbers in an array. It's for finding the Optimal Value for a property.

Problem: "Minimum capacity needed to ship packages within D days?"
Possible? No Possible? Yes
We search for the boundary between "False" and "True".

lessons.title: 6.7 Recursion: The Stack Frame
lessons.content:
Thinking Recursively

Base Case

Stop when the problem is trivial.

if (n == 0) return 1;

Recursive Step

Break problem into smaller sub-problems.

return n * fact(n-1);
Warning: Deep recursion causes Stack Overflow. Use Iteration for simple cases.

lessons.title: Lab: String Manipulation Patterns
lessons.content:
Lab: Anagrams & Palindromes

Classic Interview Questions.

Tasks

  • 1. Valid Palindrome: Check if a string is a palindrome, ignoring non-alphanumeric chars. (Use Two Pointers).
  • 2. Group Anagrams: Given ["eat", "tea", "tan", "ate", "nat", "bat"], group them. (Use Hash Map with Sorted Key).
  • 3. Longest Substring No Repeats: "abcabcbb" -> 3. (Use Sliding Window + Set).

lessons.title: 7.1 Classes vs Structs: Encapsulation
lessons.content:
The Blueprint of Objects

Encapsulation is bundling data and methods that operate on that data.

struct (Default: Public)

struct Point {
  int x; // Public by default
};

Use for "Data Bags" (PODs).

class (Default: Private)

class Player {
  int hp; // Private by default
public:
  void heal();
};

Use for "Invariant Management".

lessons.title: 7.2 Constructors, Destructors & The Rule of Zero
lessons.content:
The Lifecycle

Ctor()
Called when object is born. Initialize invariants here.
~Dtor()
Called when object dies (Scope exit). Cleanup resources.

The Rule of Zero

If your class just holds values (int, string, vector, unique_ptr), you DO NOT need to write a destructor, copy constructor, or move constructor. The compiler generates them perfectly.

Only write destructors if you are managing a raw resource (like a specific OS handle).

lessons.title: 7.3 Member Initializer Lists
lessons.content:
Assignment (Inefficient)

class Entity {
    string name;
    Entity(string n) {
        name = n; 
        // 1. name Default Constructed ""
        // 2. name Assigned "n" (Copy)
    }
};

Initialization List (Efficient)

class Entity {
    string name;
    Entity(string n) : name(n) {
        // 1. name Constructed with "n"
        // No extra step.
    }
};
Always use Initializer Lists. It is mandatory for references and const members.

lessons.title: 7.4 This, Const Methods & Mutable
lessons.content:
Const Correctness in Classes

If a method does not change the internal state, mark it const. This allows it to be called on const objects.

class Server {
  int port;
  mutable int debugCount;
public:
  int getPort() const {
    debugCount++; // OK because mutable
    return port;
  }
};

lessons.title: 7.5 Static Members & The Singleton Trap
lessons.content:
Shared State

Static Members

They belong to the Class, not the Object. Like a global variable, but namespaced.

class User {
  static int userCount;
};

int User::userCount = 0; // Define in .cpp

lessons.title: 7.6 Operator Overloading: Math Vectors
lessons.content:
Making Types behave like Primitives

C++ allows you to define +, -, *, ==, and even << for your classes.

// Vector Addition
Vec2 operator+(const Vec2& lhs, const Vec2& rhs) {
    return Vec2(lhs.x + rhs.x, lhs.y + rhs.y);
}
// Stream Insertion
std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& os, const Vec2& v) {
    os << "(" << v.x << ", " << v.y << ")";
    return os;
}

lessons.title: 7.7 Friend Classes & Encapsulation Leaks
lessons.content:
Friends with Benefits (and Risks)

A friend declaration grants full access to private members. Use sparingly. Useful for:

  • Unit Tests (accessing internals).
  • Closely related classes (Node and LinkedList).
  • Operator overloading (accessing private data).

lessons.title: 7.8 Lab: RPG Character System
lessons.content:
Lab: RPG Character

Encapsulating Stats, Inventory, and Combat Logic.

Project Specification

Design a class Character that manages HP, MP, and a list of Item objects.

Requirements

  • 1. Invariants HP cannot exceed MaxHP. HP cannot be negative. Implement takeDamage(int).
  • 2. Operator Overloading Overload << to print character stats card.
  • 3. Rule of Zero Use std::vector<Item> for inventory so you don't need manual destructors.

lessons.title: 8.1 Inheritance Hierarchy & Access
lessons.content:
The IS-A Relationship

Inheritance allows a class to derive features from a base class.

public:

Accessible by everyone.

protected:

Accessible by children.

private:

Accessible by NO ONE (except self).

lessons.title: 8.2 Virtual Functions & Runtime Polymorphism
lessons.content:
The Dynamic Dispatch

Without Virtual

Base* b = new Derived();
b->speak(); // Calls Base::speak()!

Static Binding (Compile Time).

With Virtual

class Base {
  virtual void speak();
};
b->speak(); // Calls Derived::speak()!

Dynamic Binding (Runtime).

lessons.title: 8.3 The V-Table: Under the Hood
lessons.content:
The Hidden Pointer

How does the computer know which function to call? It adds a secret pointer to every object: vptr.

Object (Heap)

vptr -> 0x5000
int data = 42;

V-Table (Static Memory)

0x5000
Shape::draw()
0x5008
Shape::area()
Calling a virtual function requires: 1. Fetch vptr. 2. Look up function address. 3. Jump. This is slightly slower than a normal call (Direct Jump).

lessons.title: 8.4 Abstract Classes & Interfaces
lessons.content:
Pure Virtual Functions

If a class has at least one pure virtual function, it is Abstract. You cannot instantiate it. 

class IShape {
public:
    virtual void draw() = 0; // Must be implemented by children
    
    virtual ~IShape() {} // Always virtual destructor!
};
This behaves like an interface in Java or C#.

lessons.title: 8.5 Virtual Destructors (Critical)
lessons.content:
The Memory Leak Trap

If you delete a Derived object through a Base pointer, and the Base destructor is NOT virtual... The Derived destructor never runs.

Base* b = new Derived();
delete b;
// ~Base() called.
// ~Derived() NOT called! Memory Leak!

lessons.title: 8.6 Casting: dynamic_cast & static_cast
lessons.content:
static_cast

  • Compile-time check.
  • Fast (0 overhead).
  • Unsafe for Downcasting (Base -> Derived) if you aren't 100% sure.

dynamic_cast

  • Runtime check (RTTI).
  • Slow (Traverses inheritance tree).
  • Returns nullptr if cast fails.

lessons.title: 8.7 Multiple Inheritance & Diamond Problem
lessons.content:
The Diamond of Death

Grandparent
Parent A
Parent B
Child

Child has TWO copies of Grandparent. Solved by virtual inheritance.

lessons.title: 8.8 Lab: Entity Component System (ECS)
lessons.content:
Lab: Entity Component System

Composition over Inheritance.

Project Specification

Deep inheritance trees are bad for game development. Instead, we use Composition. Build a system where an Entity is just an ID, and Components (Position, Sprite, Health) are attached to it.

Core Architecture

  • Base Component struct Component { virtual ~Component() = default; };
  • Storage std::vector<unique_ptr<Component>> attached to Entity.
  • Dynamic Cast Use dynamic_cast or `typeid` to retrieve specific components (e.g., getComponent<Health>()).

lessons.title: 9.1 Auto & Type Inference
lessons.content:
Almost Always Auto (AAA)

Let the compiler deduce the type. It never makes a mistake.

Before C++11

std::vector<std::string>::iterator it = vec.begin();

After C++11

auto it = vec.begin();
Note: auto decays references and const by default!
Use auto& or const auto& if you want a reference.

lessons.title: 9.2 Range-based For Loops
lessons.content:
Syntactic Sugar

for (int x : vec) { ... } // Copies each element
for (int& x : vec) { ... } // Modifies elements
for (const int& x : vec) { ... } // Read only (Best Practice)

lessons.title: 9.3 Lambda Expressions
lessons.content:
Anonymous Functions

Functions inside functions. Essential for algorithms and callbacks.

[captures] (params) { body }
[=] : Capture all by value (Copy)
[&] : Capture all by reference
[x] : Capture x by value
auto isEven = [](int n) { return n % 2 == 0; };

lessons.title: 9.4 Functional Paradigms: std::function
lessons.content:
First Class Functions

std::function is a type-erased wrapper that can store ANY callable target (Lambda, Function Pointer, Functor). 

#include <functional>

void sortData(std::vector<int>& data, std::function<bool(int, int)> comparator) {
    // ...
}
Performance Warning: std::function has virtual call overhead and may allocate memory. For tight loops, use Templates instead!

lessons.title: 9.5 Move Semantics & std::move
lessons.content:
Stealing Resources

Moving is just a shallow copy followed by nullifying the source. It turns O(N) copies into O(1) pointer swaps.

L-value vs R-value

  • L-value: Has a name, has an address. (int x)
  • R-value: Temporary, no name. (5, x + y, function())

std::move(x)

It actually does NOTHING at runtime. It just casts x to an R-value reference T&&, telling the compiler "You can plunder this object".

lessons.title: 9.6 Smart Pointers Recap
lessons.content:
Modern Memory

Never use new and delete again.

std::make_unique<T>()
std::make_shared<T>()
(Covered extensively in Module 3. This lesson reviews them in the context of C++11/14 adoption.)

lessons.title: 9.7 Chrono & Random
lessons.content:
std::chrono

Type-safe time library.

auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
// ...
auto end = ...;
auto ms = duration_cast<milliseconds>(end - start);

std::random

Better than rand().

std::mt19937 mt(seed);
std::uniform_int_distribution<int> dist(1, 100);
int roll = dist(mt);

lessons.title: 9.8 Lab: Async Event System
lessons.content:
Lab: Event Bus

Using Lambdas and std::function to drive architecture.

Build a pub/sub system where systems can subscribe to events (like "PlayerHit") using lambdas.

// Usage
Events::subscribe("PlayerHit", [](Event& e) {
    playSound("ouch.wav");
});

Events::publish("PlayerHit", {10});

lessons.title: 10.1 C++17: Filesystem & Optional
lessons.content:
std::filesystem

Finally, a standard way to walk directories. 

using fs = std::filesystem;
for(auto& p : fs::directory_iterator(".")) {
    cout << p.path() << endl;
}

std::optional

Expressing "May or may not contain a value" without pointers. 

std::optional<int> findUser(int id) {
    if (!exists(id)) return std::nullopt;
    return 42;
}

lessons.title: 10.2 C++20: Concepts & Constraints
lessons.content:
Taming Templates

Concepts allow you to constrain template parameters, getting clear error messages instead of 500 pages of "template substitution failed".

The Old Way (SFINAE)
template <typename T, typename = std::enable_if_t<is_integral_v<T>>>
The C++20 Way
template <std::integral T>
void add(T a, T b) { ... }

Can also define your own concepts like HasDrawMethod<T>.

lessons.title: 10.3 C++20: Ranges & Views
lessons.content:
Pipeline Programming

Think UNIX pipes | but for C++ containers. Lazy evaluation included. 

#include <ranges>

auto result = nums 
    | std::views::filter([](int n){ return n % 2 == 0; })
    | std::views::transform([](int n){ return n * n; })
    | std::views::take(5);
No temporary vectors created
Completely lazy

lessons.title: 10.4 C++20: Coroutines
lessons.content:
Functions that Pause

A coroutine is a function that can suspend execution to be resumed later. Essential for Async I/O and Generators.

co_await socket.read();
// Function sleeps here.
// Thread goes to do other work.
process(data);
Generator<int> sequence() {
  for(int i=0;;++i)
    co_yield i;
}

lessons.title: 10.5 C++20: Modules (The Future)
lessons.content:
Death to Headers

#include is literal copy-paste. It is slow and causes conflicts. Modules import pre-compiled binary interfaces.

Old
#include <iostream>
New
import std.core;

Note: Compiler support is still maturing (2026). CMake supports it now.

lessons.title: 10.6 The Spaceship Operator <=>
lessons.content:
Three-way Comparison

<=>

Instead of writing operator==, !=, <, >, <=, >=... you write just ONE. 

auto operator<=>(const MyClass&) const = default;

The compiler auto-generates all 6 comparison operators for you by comparing members in order.

lessons.title: 10.7 Constexpr & Consteval
lessons.content:
Compile-Time Execution

constexpr

Can run at compile time IF inputs are constant. Otherwise runs at runtime.

consteval (C++20)

MUST run at compile time. Error if it can't.

Metaprogramming replacement

You can now write normal functions (loops, if-statements) and run them during compilation to generate lookup tables. No more template insanity.

lessons.title: 10.8 Lab: Modern Library Design
lessons.content:
Lab: Modern Core Lib

Using Concepts, Ranges, and Modules.

Project Specification

Create a small utility library for "Safe Arrays" that feels like a native part of the language.

Checklist

  • 1. Concepts Constraint template types to only std::is_arithmetic (Numbers).
  • 2. Spaceship Implement operator<=> using default.
  • 3. Ranges Add a method that returns a view (e.g., array elements multiplied by 2).

lessons.title: 11.1 Exceptions: The Good, Bad, and Ugly
lessons.content:
Error Handling Models

Return Codes (C-Style)

int ret = func();
if (ret != 0) handle();

Fast, but ignores are fatal. Clutters code.

Exceptions (C++)

try { func(); }
catch (e) { handle(); }

Clean happy path. Slow on error. Needs RTTI.

lessons.title: 11.2 The Strong Exception Guarantee
lessons.content:
Transaction Safety

Commit or Rollback

If a function throws an exception, the program state should remain unchanged.

// The "Copy and Swap" Idiom
void append(Data new_data) {
    auto temp = current_data; // 1. Copy (May Throw)
    temp.push_back(new_data); // 2. Modify Temp (May Throw)
    
    current_data.swap(temp);  // 3. Swap (Noexcept - Safe!)
}
If step 1 or 2 fails, 'current_data' is untouched. If step 3 runs, it succeeds instantly.

lessons.title: 11.3 noexcept: The Optimization Hint
lessons.content:
Moving Fast

std::vector will NOT use your Move Constructor during a resize unless it is marked noexcept.

Safety reasons: If moving element 5 fails halfway, we can't restore the old list. Copying is safer. 

MyClass(MyClass&& other) noexcept {
    // ...
}

lessons.title: 11.4 std::expected (C++23)
lessons.content:
The Best of Both Worlds

Return a Value OR an Error. Like Rust's Result type.

std::expected<int, string> divide(int a, int b) {
if (b == 0) return std::unexpected("Div by zero");
return a / b;
}
auto result = divide(10, 0);
if (result) cout << *result;
else cerr << result.error();

lessons.title: 11.5 Unit Testing with GTest
lessons.content:
Google Test Framework

The industry standard for C++ testing. Macros galore.

TEST(MathTests, Addition) {
  EXPECT_EQ(add(2, 2), 4);
  EXPECT_GT(add(10, 5), 10);
}

Integrates easily with CMake (FetchContent).

lessons.title: 11.6 Sanitizers: ASAN & UBSAN
lessons.content:
Finding Invisible Bugs

Address Sanitizer (ASAN)

Detects Buffer Overflows, Use-After-Free.

-fsanitize=address

Undefined Behavior (UBSAN)

Detects Integer Overflows, Null dereferences.

-fsanitize=undefined

lessons.title: 11.7 Lab: Robust Parser
lessons.content:
Lab: Bulletproof Parser

Handling corrupt data gracefully.

Project Specification

Write a CSV parser that never crashes, no matter how garbage the input file is.

Error Scenarios

  • File does not exist (Exception/Optional).
  • Missing columns in a row (Log & Skip).
  • Integer column contains "Banana" (Parse Error).
Goal: The program must print a summary report of valid rows vs rejected rows, instead of aborting.

lessons.title: 12.1 CPU Architecture: Hierarchy of Speed
lessons.content:
Distance Matters

Accessing RAM is slow. Accessing Disk is glacial. The CPU only works fast on registers and L1 Cache.

Registers (0ns)
L1 Cache (1ns)
L2 Cache (4ns)
L3 Cache (10ns)
RAM (100ns) - 100x Slower!

lessons.title: 12.2 Data Oriented Design (DOD)
lessons.content:
Structure of Arrays (SoA)

OOP (AoS)

struct Particle {
    float x, y, z;
    int color;
};
vector<Particle> parts;

Padding bytes waste space. Mixed types pollute cache line.

DOD (SoA)

struct Particles {
    vector<float> x;
    vector<float> y;
    vector<float> z;
    vector<int> color;
};

Packed tightly. SIMD ready.

lessons.title: 12.3 Cache Locality & Prefetching
lessons.content:
Linked List vs Vector

Iterating a std::list is often 10x-50x slower than std::vector simply because of cache misses (pointer chasing).

vs
Rule of Thumb: Always default to std::vector. Only use Node-based containers if you strictly need O(1) insertion in the middle AND iterators stability.

lessons.title: 12.4 Branch Prediction
lessons.content:
Pipeline Hazards

CPUs guess which way an if statement will go. If they guess wrong, they flush the pipeline (expensive).

// Sorting data makes processing faster!
if (data[i] > 128) sum += data[i];

If data is random: 50% misprediction rate.
If data is sorted: 0% misprediction rate (pattern is TTTTTFFFFF).

[[likely]] and [[unlikely]] (C++20)

lessons.title: 12.5 Auto-Vectorization & SIMD
lessons.content:
Single Instruction Multiple Data

Processing 4 or 8 floats in a SINGLE CPU cycle.

A1
A2
A3
A4
+ (One Cycle)
B1
B2
B3
B4

lessons.title: 12.6 Profiling Tools: Perf & Flamegraphs
lessons.content:
Don't Guess. Measure.

Premature optimization is the root of all evil.

Linux Perf

perf record -g ./app

Samples stack traces thousands of times per second.

FlameGraphs

Visualizing where time is spent. Wide bars = Slow functions.

lessons.title: 12.7 Lab: N-Body Physics Simulation
lessons.content:
Lab: Optimization Challenge

Make it run 100x faster.

Project Specification

Start with a naive O(N^2) physics loop implementing gravity between 10,000 particles.

Optimization Steps

  • 1. Baseline Measure FPS with std::vector<Particle>.
  • 2. SoA Transformation Convert to Structure of Arrays to assist vectorization.
  • 3. Multi-Threading Use std::for_each(std::execution::par_unseq).

lessons.title: 13.1 Threading Basics: std::thread
lessons.content:
Parallel Execution

Concurrency is dealing with a lot of things at once. Parallelism is doing a lot of things at once.

#include <thread>

void task(int id) { ... }

int main() {
  std::thread t1(task, 1);
  std::thread t2(task, 2);
  // These run SIMULTANEOUSLY on different cores.
  t1.join(); // Wait for t1
  t2.join(); // Wait for t2
}

lessons.title: 13.2 Data Races & Mutexes
lessons.content:
The Data Race

When two threads read/write the same memory at the same time without synchronization. Result: Undefined Behavior.

Thread A: x++; (Read 5, Write 6)
Thread B: x++; (Read 5, Write 6)
Result: x is 6, not 7!

The Mutex Lock

std::mutex ensures mutual exclusion. Only one thread can hold the lock.

std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mu);
x++; // Safe zone

lessons.title: 13.3 Deadlocks & Lock Guards
lessons.content:
The Deadly Embrace

Thread A
❌
Thread B

Thread A holds Lock 1, waits for Lock 2.
Thread B holds Lock 2, waits for Lock 1.
Freeze forever.

Solution: Always acquire locks in the same order. Or use std::scoped_lock(l1, l2) (C++17) which avoids deadlocks automatically.

lessons.title: 13.4 Condition Variables
lessons.content:
Waiting Efficiently

Don't spin in a loop while(!ready) {}. It burns CPU. Use std::condition_variable to sleep until notified.

Consumer Thread:
std::unique_lock lock(mu);
cv.wait(lock, []{ return !queue.empty(); });
// Awake! Queue has data.
Producer Thread:
queue.push(data);
cv.notify_one(); // Wake up consumer

lessons.title: 13.5 Atomics & Lock-Free Programming
lessons.content:
Going Lock-Free

Mutexes are slow (syscalls). Atomics use special CPU instructions (Compare-And-Swap) to modify memory safely without sleeping.

std::atomic<int>

counter++; // Thread safe!

Maps to `lock xadd` assembly.

Memory Ordering

memory_order_relaxed
memory_order_seq_cst

Deep magic. Only for experts.

lessons.title: 13.6 std::async & Futures
lessons.content:
High Level Concurrency

Stop managing thread lifetimes manually. Just ask the system to "Run this function and give me the result later". 

// Start async task
std::future<int> result = std::async(std::launch::async, heavy_computation);

// Do other work...

// Get result (Blocks if not ready)
int value = result.get();

lessons.title: 13.7 Parallel Algorithms (C++17)
lessons.content:
Multi-Core Sorting

In C++17, you can parallelize standard algorithms with ONE extra argument.

Traditional
std::sort(v.begin(), v.end());
Parallel (uses all cores)
std::sort(std::execution::par, v.begin(), v.end());

lessons.title: 13.8 Lab: Thread Pool Implementation
lessons.content:
Lab: The Thread Pool

The most common concurrency pattern in the world.

Project Specification

Creating threads is expensive (OS overhead). Design a system with N worker threads that pop tasks from a queue and execute them efficiently.

Architecture

  • Workers: vector<thread> looping forever.
  • Task Queue: queue<function<void()>>.
  • Sync: mutex for queue access, condition_variable to wake workers.
  • Shutdown: A robust way to stop all threads gracefully (join).

lessons.title: 14.1 Stream I/O: cin and cout
lessons.content:
Streams Abstraction

C++ treats input and output as a stream of bytes. cin and cout are just global objects connected to standard input/output.

// Reading unknown number of inputs
int x;
while (std::cin >> x) {
    // Loop runs until EOF (Ctrl+D / Ctrl+Z) or Invalid Input type
    process(x);
}

lessons.title: 14.2 File Streams (fstream)
lessons.content:
Writing (ofstream)

std::ofstream file("data.txt");
if (file.is_open()) {
    file << "Hello World\n";
    file << 42;
}
// Closed automatically (RAII)

Reading (ifstream)

std::ifstream file("data.txt");
std::string line;
while (std::getline(file, line)) {
    cout << line << endl;
}

lessons.title: 14.3 Binary vs Text Files
lessons.content:
Serialization

Text: Human readable (ASCII/UTF8). Needs parsing. Slow.
Binary: Valid memory dump. Machine readable. Fast.

file.write(reinterpret_cast<char*>(&myStruct), sizeof(myStruct));
Danger: Binary files are not portable across different architectures (Endianness, Padding, 32 vs 64 bit).

lessons.title: 14.4 String Streams (stringstream)
lessons.content:
In-Memory Formatting

Treat a string like a file. Useful for splitting sentences or formatting numbers.

std::string data = "User: 100 20.5";
std::stringstream ss(data);

std::string label;
int id;
float score;

ss >> label >> id >> score;
// label="User:", id=100, score=20.5

lessons.title: 14.5 CLI Arguments (argc, argv)
lessons.content:
Command Line Interface

./app --verbose --input data.csv
int main(int argc, char* argv[])
  • argc: Argument Count (3 in example above).
  • argv[0]: Program Name ("./app").
  • argv[1]: "--verbose".

lessons.title: 14.6 Environment & System Calls
lessons.content:
Environment Variables

char* path = std::getenv("PATH");
if (path) cout << path;

System Execution

system("ls -l > out.txt");
// Executes shell command. Risky!

lessons.content:

Reading structured data from disk.

lessons.title: 15.1 Graph Representation: Adjacency List vs Matrix
lessons.content:
Graphs in C++

A graph is just nodes and edges. How you store it determines your algorithm's speed.

Adjacency Matrix

int adj[N][N];
// adj[u][v] = 1 if edge exists.

Space: O(V^2). Bad for sparse graphs.
Lookup: O(1). Fast.

Adjacency List (Standard)

vector<int> adj[N];
// adj[u] contains {v1, v2, ...}

Space: O(V + E). Perfect for CP.
Lookup: O(Degree).

lessons.title: 15.2 BFS & DFS Templates
lessons.content:
BFS (Breadth First)

Shortest Path in unweighted graphs. Flood fill.

queue<int> q;
vis[start] = true;
q.push(start);
while (!q.empty()) { ... }

DFS (Depth First)

Maze solving, Cycle detection, Topology.

void dfs(int u) {
  vis[u] = true;
  for (int v : adj[u]) dfs(v);
}

lessons.title: 15.3 Dijkstra's Algorithm (Shortest Path)
lessons.content:
The Priority Queue Trick

priority_queue<pair<int,int>, vector<pair<int,int>>, greater<>> pq;

dist[start] = 0;
pq.push({0, start});

while (!pq.empty()) {
    int d = pq.top().first;
    int u = pq.top().second;
    pq.pop();

    if (d > dist[u]) continue; // Important Optimization

    for (auto& edge : adj[u]) {
        if (dist[u] + weight < dist[v]) {
            dist[v] = dist[u] + weight;
            pq.push({dist[v], v});
        }
    }
}
Uses std::greater to make it a Min-Heap (smallest distance on top).

lessons.title: 15.4 Disjoint Set Union (DSU)
lessons.content:
Union-Find

The ultimate data structure for connectivity queries. "Are these two friends connected?" "Merge these two groups."

find(i):
Path Compression (make all nodes point to root). O(alpha(N)).
union(i, j):
Union by Rank/Size (attach small tree to big tree).
int find(int i) { if (parent[i] == i) return i; return parent[i] = find(parent[i]); // Recurse & Compress }

lessons.title: 15.5 Dynamic Programming (DP) Patterns
lessons.content:
Memoization (Top-Down)

Recursive + Table.

int fib(int n) {
    if (memo[n] != -1) return memo[n];
    return memo[n] = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2);
}

Tabulation (Bottom-Up)

Iterative + Table. Better cache locality.

dp[0] = 0; dp[1] = 1;
for (int i=2; i<=n; ++i) {
    dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2];
}

lessons.title: 15.6 Bit Manipulation for CP
lessons.content:
Bitwise Magic

Check if odd if (n & 1)
Multiply by 2 n << 1
Divide by 2 n >> 1
Power of 2 check !(n & (n-1))
Set bit K n | (1 << k)

lessons.title: 15.7 Number Theory Snippets
lessons.content:
GCD (Euclid)

return b == 0 ? a : gcd(b, a % b);

Built-in: std::gcd (C++17)

Modular Exponentiation

Calculate (a^b) % m in O(log b).

long long binpow(long long a, long long b) {
    long long res = 1;
    while (b > 0) {
        if (b & 1) res = res * a;
        a = a * a;
        b >>= 1;
    }
    return res;
}

lessons.title: 16.1 The Build Pipeline: From Code to Binary
lessons.content:
De-Mystifying the Linker

The Process: Compilation is a multi-stage pipeline. Errors can happen at any stage, and their error messages look completely different.

1. Preprocessor

Text Replacement.

#include, #define

2. Compiler

Code -> ASM.

Syntax Errors, Type Check.

3. Assembler

ASM -> Object (.o)

Machine Code Generation.

4. Linker

Combine .o -> Exe

Unresolved Symbols.

Symbol Resolution

main.o
main()
CALL _Z3fooV (Unknown)
+
utils.o
_Z3fooV: (Address 0x100)
...instructions...
➜
app.exe
CALL 0x100

lessons.title: 16.2 CMake Basics: Targets & Properties
lessons.content:
Modern CMake Philosophy

Stop thinking in terms of directories and flags. Think in terms of Targets (Executables and Libraries) and Requirements.

Old CMake (Avoid)
include_directories(include)
link_libraries(math)
Globals are bad. This applies flags to EVERYTHING, even tests that don't need them.
Modern CMake (Good)
add_library(math src/math.cpp)
target_include_directories(math PUBLIC include)
Encapsulation. Anyone who links 'math' automatically gets the include path.

lessons.title: 16.3 Static (.a) vs Shared (.so/.dll) Libraries
lessons.content:
Static Library (.a / .lib)

Code is copied directly into your executable at compile time.

  • ✅ No "DLL missing" errors.
  • ✅ Faster execution (Link Time Optimization).
  • ❌ Large executable size.
  • ❌ Must recompile app to update library.

Shared Library (.so / .dll)

Code sits in a separate file. Loaded by OS at runtime.

  • ✅ Small executable size.
  • ✅ Plugins (swap DLLs without recompiling).
  • ❌ "DLL Hell" (Version conflicts).
  • ❌ Slower startup (Dynamic Loader overhead).

lessons.title: 16.4 Dependency Management: Vcpkg & Conan
lessons.content:
The Package Manager Revolution

For 30 years, C++ had no npm install. You had to download zips and build them manually. Now we have vcpkg (Microsoft) and Conan.

# Converting a project to vcpkg
vcpkg install fmt
// In CMakeLists.txt
find_package(fmt CONFIG REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(main PRIVATE fmt::fmt)
Works across Windows, Linux, and Mac. No more "It works on my machine".

lessons.title: 16.5 C++ Interop: Python & C APIs
lessons.content:
Speaking to Aliens

extern "C"

C++ mangles names (e.g., _Z3fooi) to support overloading. C does not. To call C++ from C (or Python/Rust), you must disable mangling. 

#ifdef __cplusplus
extern "C" {
#endif

    // This function name will stay "calculate" in the binary
    void calculate(int x);

#ifdef __cplusplus
}
#endif

lessons.title: 16.6 Lab: Modern CMake Project
lessons.content:
Lab: Professional Build System

Structure a project like a Senior Engineer.

Project Specification

Structure a multi-library project using "Modern CMake" (Target-Based) philosophy.

Directory Layout

root/
├── CMakeLists.txt
├── app/
│   ├── CMakeLists.txt
│   └── main.cpp
├── core/
│   ├── CMakeLists.txt
│   ├── include/math.h
│   └── src/math.cpp
└── tests/
    ├── CMakeLists.txt
    └── test_main.cpp

Target-Centered Design

# core/CMakeLists.txt
add_library(core STATIC src/math.cpp)
target_include_directories(core PUBLIC include)

# app/CMakeLists.txt
add_executable(app main.cpp)
target_link_libraries(app PRIVATE core)
